speaker: interviewer
text: Look like, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: If it's important to show both, we'll show both pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because this-- this is-- this is, to me, this is a-- this is a picture of my mother and father in France, [? in ?] [? Fiblen, ?] when they were the head of an orphanage. And this was taken in 1946, in France.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name, what is your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dora Fried Josipovits, and Ignatz Josipovits.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dr. Ignatz Josipovits. This is me, Handa Josipovits, pouting a little bit. And I was two-year-old-- two-years-old. This was in Sevlus. Velky Sevlus, actually. The part where I was born has three names. So sometimes it gets a little mixed up. But it's all the same. And this was in 1926. This is the picture of my-- of the wedding of my uncle, who came home from America, and my grandmother married him off. And this is the whole family, aunts and uncles, grandma and grandpa, and cousins. And with the exception of about five, they all perished in the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where--

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was also taken in Sevlus. And I was 12-years-old. So I was born in '24. It's '36, 1936.

speaker: interviewer
text: In this picture, in the lower row, in the middle, that's me. I was 16 years old. And the other five girls are my schoolmates. This was just before we were deported to Poland. '40, '41? In '41. In Sevlus. And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sevlus.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Sevlus?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We all went to the business school. This is my brother, Sam. And he was named after my great-- our great-grandfather. And this picture was taken before the war, before they took him to the-- to the forced labor camp. And he was about 20. This is also in Sevlus.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date, about? Do you remember what it would be? [SIGHS] Approximately. Uh, about '42. This picture was taken in [? Fiblen, ?] where I worked in an orphanage. And the two children with me in the picture, the one standing to my right, her name is [? Basha ?].

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was adopted by an American family. And the little girl that I'm holding was also adopted by a [? Mr. Gartenberg, ?] an American man. And she lives now in Jerusalem, and has six children. And she went back to school. And she learned how to be a nurse. And her name is Eva Margolis.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was very sad. Because when I met her, she kept asking, who am I? How did you get this name for me? Where do I come from? She's searching for her roots. She went back to France, to the organization.

speaker: interviewee
text: They brought her out from Poland. She's very orthodox. And she wants to marry her children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they ask who she is. And she has no clue whatsoever. She can't find, nowheres, anything that, where does she come from? But I assured her that she's-- she is Jewish. She shouldn't have to-- She doesn't have to worry. Because she has such [INAUDIBLE], you know? Such that only a Jewish child would be like that. She's very sweet. So you reassured her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was taken about what time?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was take in '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eva Margolis, the little girl that I spoke about before, is the-- the young woman at the head of the table, to the left-- to the right of the man. And these are her 6 wonderful children. They live in Jerusalem. And this picture was take-- taken about six-- six years ago, in 87, maybe. And by-- by now, probably, she's a grandmother. But she's happily married. And she's a wonderful person. And she's a nurse now. [? So ?] [? the ?] [? end of that. ?] This is the bus that brought the children to [? Fiblen, ?] to the orphanage where I worked in France. And the three little-- in the front are three little girls. Those are the-- One of them is Eva Margolis. And they all were very sweet. And some of them were adopted to America. And the rest went to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have to repeat it. Oh. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait till they're away. [BACKGROUND NOISE] This picture was taken in 1946. '46, yes. This picture is my husband and I on our wedding. And this was taken May 17, 1949, in Santiago de Chile. But just-- This is my mother and father, Dora Josipovits and Dr. Ignatz Josipovits. And this picture was taken in Los Angeles, about nine years ago. This picture was taken in 1985. This picture is the picture taken of the family on Suzy-- our daughter, youngest daughter's, Suzy's wedding, in June, 13-- on-- on June 13 in Ness Ziona, Israel. And on the picture, from right-- from left to right, is myself, my-- in the first row, myself, my husband, my grandson, my-- Josh, my granddaughter, Michal, my daughter, Rosie, my daughter-in-law, Robin [? Nana ?] [? Rushka. ?] And in front of us is standing, Ben-- from left to right, Benjamin-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: No? Yitzhak Menache, and Joseph.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are they in those pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: Benjamin is six. Yitzhak Menache is two. And-- and Joseph is five. In the back row is my son-in-law, Ron Silverstein, my nephew, Dr. David Stark, and my son, Allen Stark. This is our daughter, Suzy, and our son-in-law, Chaim. And their name is Serenevits. They were married June 13, 1994. And we hope they will live together, [INAUDIBLE], in good health, and happiness, and have many children. And they live in Israel. These are the youngs-- youngest of the Stark grandchildren. This is the oldest one. The older one is Matthis Yahu Zvi. And the younger one-- no, I have it confused, sorry. The oldest one, Yitzhak Menache. And the youngest is Matthis Yahu Zvi. And these are Allen, our son's, children. And they live in Santa Barbara. And this picture was taken in 1994.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know, it's a terrible [? thing. ?] Now, we have a beautiful family. Tell me your name. My name is Handa Stark. This is my husband, Andrew Stark. This is my grandson, Joshua-- Joseph Moshe. And how old are you, Joseph? JOSEPH (GRANDSON): Five. Five. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the other grandchildren, some are not at home. And one is-- he is camera shy. He doesn't want to come. So we just have to do it this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's fine. It's a beautiful picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we have beautiful, beautiful six grandchildren so far. We hope we going to have some more. ANDREW (HUSBAND): One is on the way already.

speaker: interviewer
text: One is on the way? [LAUGHS] ANDREW (HUSBAND): Yeah. Yeah, good. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, very nice. And again, I thank you for taking the interview.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my pleasure to talk to you and to tell you my story. And I hope when my grandchildren, or my children and grandchildren will have a chance to see it, or a long, long time from now, when we are not here anymore, then they get something out of it. That they can here their Bubbe talk and tell her story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, it was my pleasure to hear your story. It was wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. ANDREW (HUSBAND): And the children going to show it to their grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: We hope. We hope. ANDREW (HUSBAND): Let's hope. It's a wonderful thought. JOSEPH (GRANDSON): Bubbe.
